prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that sea otters help kelp forests by

choices.text: providing the sunlight that the growing kelp needs
choices.rationale: Sunlight comes from the sun, not from otters.

choices.text: keeping the population of kelp-grazing urchins in check
choices.rationale: Correct. When otters vanish, urchins multiply and graze the kelp down, so otters limit urchins.

choices.text: slowing the rapid growth of the kelp near the surface
choices.rationale: Otters do not slow kelp growth; their loss harms the kelp.

choices.text: anchoring the giant kelp firmly to the rocky seafloor
choices.rationale: The kelp anchors itself; otters do not anchor it.

explanation: Otters help by controlling the urchins that would otherwise destroy the kelp.

stimulus.title: Forests Beneath the Waves
stimulus.text: Off some cool, rocky coasts grow forests that no hiker will ever walk through. Giant kelp, anchored to the seafloor, can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface, swaying in the current like trees in wind. These kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters, much as a woodland shelters birds and deer. The kelp grows astonishingly fast, sometimes half a meter in a single day, drinking sunlight near the surface. But the forest is fragile. Where otters vanish, sea urchins multiply and graze the kelp down to bare rock, leaving a barren the divers call an urchin desert. A single missing animal can unmake a whole forest.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the author regards folk weather signs as

choices.text: a mix of useful observation and unreliable belief
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says folk knowledge mixed keen observation with superstition.

choices.text: purely superstition with no basis in real conditions
choices.rationale: Some signs, like the hazy moon, reflect real conditions.

choices.text: entirely useless and worth completely ignoring
choices.rationale: The passage credits some folk signs with real wisdom.

choices.text: more accurate than any modern forecasting method
choices.rationale: The passage says forecasting absorbed the best parts, implying it is reliable.

explanation: The author views folk signs as a blend of insight and superstition.

stimulus.title: Reading the Weather Before Radio
stimulus.text: Long before satellites mapped storms from orbit, farmers learned to read the sky and the land for signs of coming weather. A ring of haze around the moon, the smell of soil before rain, the behavior of swallows flying low: such clues were gathered into almanacs and passed down through families. Much of this folk knowledge was unreliable, mixing keen observation with superstition. Yet some of it held real wisdom, since the conditions that produce a hazy moon truly can precede rain. Modern forecasting has not so much replaced this older watching as absorbed its best parts, trading the rooster's crow for the radar's sweep.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that seeds wear hooks in order to

choices.text: catch onto animals that carry them to new places
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says hooked seeds snag passing animals and hitch a ride.

choices.text: make the seeds heavier so they fall straight down
choices.rationale: Hooks aid travel, not added weight to fall straight down.

choices.text: protect the seeds from being eaten by any animal
choices.rationale: Hooks help seeds catch a ride, not avoid being eaten.

choices.text: help the seeds spin like helicopters in the wind
choices.rationale: Spinning like a helicopter describes winged seeds, not hooked ones.

explanation: Hooks let seeds catch onto animals for transport.

stimulus.title: Travelers Without Legs
stimulus.text: A plant cannot walk, yet its seeds must somehow leave the shadow of the parent and find open ground. Evolution has answered this need with a surprising range of tricks. Some seeds grow papery wings and spin away on the wind like tiny helicopters. Others wear hooks that snag the fur of passing animals, hitching a ride for miles. Still others hide inside sweet fruit, counting on an animal to eat them and carry them off, depositing them far away. Each method is a kind of bargain struck with the world around the plant. The seed that travels farthest is often the one with the best chance to grow where the parent's shade cannot reach.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that railroads made local sun time a problem because trains

choices.text: were unable to operate during the daylight hours at all
choices.rationale: Nothing suggests trains could not run in daylight.

choices.text: traveled too slowly for any timetable to matter to passengers
choices.rationale: The passage stresses schedules mattered greatly, not that trains were slow.

choices.text: depended on schedules that required everyone to agree on the time
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says a schedule means nothing if each station defines noon differently.

choices.text: could not run unless every town abandoned the use of clocks
choices.rationale: Standard time required shared clocks, not abandoning them.

explanation: Schedules required a shared definition of time, which local sun time prevented.

stimulus.title: When Noon Moved
stimulus.text: Before railroads, every town kept its own time, set by the sun overhead at noon. A traveler crossing the country adjusted a watch town by town, and no one minded a few minutes' difference. Railroads made the patchwork intolerable. Trains ran on schedules, and a schedule means nothing if each station defines noon differently; a missed connection could be a matter of life and death. So the railways carved the land into broad zones, each agreeing to share a single time. People grumbled at first about clocks that no longer matched the sun, but the convenience won out. The hour on the wall had become a public agreement rather than a private reading of the sky.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that a delta downstream of a large new dam would most likely

choices.text: lose land to the sea over time
choices.rationale: Correct. Dams trap sediment, starving the delta so waves carry off more land.

choices.text: remain exactly the same size forever
choices.rationale: The passage says a starved delta retreats, not stays the same.

choices.text: gain land more quickly than before
choices.rationale: Reduced sediment shrinks the delta rather than speeding growth.

choices.text: stop receiving any river water at all
choices.rationale: The dam traps sediment, not all the water.

explanation: A sediment-starved delta would lose land to the sea.

stimulus.title: Land Made of Mud
stimulus.text: A great river carries more than water; it carries soil, scoured from distant hills and ground into fine particles. Where the river finally slows and meets the sea, it can no longer hold this load, and the sediment settles out, building flat new land called a delta. Over centuries a delta can grow seaward, fanning into a maze of channels and islands. But the process depends on a steady supply of mud. When dams upstream trap that sediment, the delta is starved; waves carry off more land than the river replaces, and the coastline begins to retreat. What looks like permanent ground is, in truth, a balance constantly being rebuilt.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that customers valued Mr. Owusu's stall chiefly because they felt

choices.text: uncertain about the quality of his produce
choices.rationale: His setting aside perfect produce implies quality, not doubt.

choices.text: that his prices were the lowest available
choices.rationale: The passage explicitly says it was not his prices.

choices.text: rushed through their purchases quickly
choices.rationale: Nothing suggests customers were rushed.

choices.text: personally recognized and cared for
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says they returned because, at his stall, 'they were known.'

explanation: Customers valued feeling known and cared for.

stimulus.title: The Stall That Listened
stimulus.text: Every Saturday, Mr. Owusu set out the same vegetables on the same table, and for years his stall was just one among many. What set him apart, his daughter noticed, was not his prices but his memory. He recalled which customer's child disliked onions, who was cooking for a crowd that weekend, who had been ill and needed something gentle. He would set aside a perfect tomato for one regular and toss an extra handful of beans to another. People did not return only for the produce; they returned because, at his stall, they were known. When he finally retired, the market felt larger and lonelier, though not a single other stall had closed.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that deeper layers of an ice sheet generally contain air that is

choices.text: older than the air in shallower layers
choices.rationale: Correct. Layers settle over time, so deeper ice formed earlier and traps older air.

choices.text: younger than the air in shallower layers
choices.rationale: Deeper layers are older, not younger.

choices.text: identical in age to today's atmosphere
choices.rationale: Deeper air is from the distant past, not today.

choices.text: free of any trapped gases at all
choices.rationale: The passage says bubbles trap the gases the air carried.

explanation: Because layers accumulate over time, deeper air is older.

stimulus.title: Bubbles of Old Air
stimulus.text: Each winter's snowfall on a polar ice sheet does not entirely melt; it packs down, traps tiny pockets of air, and slowly turns to ice. Layer settles on layer, year after year, so that drilling deep into the ice is a way of drilling back through time. Trapped in those bubbles is a sample of the very atmosphere that existed when the snow fell, including the gases it carried. By analyzing cores pulled from kilometers down, researchers can compare today's air with the air of tens of thousands of years ago. The ice keeps no diary of dates, yet its layers, counted carefully, let scientists assign an age to each whisper of ancient air.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that public voting was a problem chiefly because it

choices.text: prevented neighbors from ever discussing politics
choices.rationale: Public voting did not stop political discussion.

choices.text: required voters to travel long distances to vote
choices.rationale: Travel distance is never mentioned.

choices.text: let powerful people see and punish how others voted
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says open voting invited pressure and fear of retaliation.

choices.text: made the counting of votes slow and unreliable
choices.rationale: Vote-counting speed is not discussed.

explanation: The danger of public voting was exposure to pressure and punishment.

stimulus.title: The Secret Ballot
stimulus.text: It is easy to assume voting has always been private, but for a long time it was not. In many places people voted aloud or by a show of hands, in full view of employers, landlords, and neighbors. That openness invited pressure: a worker might think twice before voting against a boss who could see the choice. Reformers pushed for a printed ballot, marked alone and dropped in a box, so that no one need fear retaliation for an honest vote. The change seemed minor, a mere matter of paper, yet it shifted real power toward the ordinary voter, who could now decide without an audience.
